new_findings.double_dipping: {"__type__":"sc_referee.checks.base.Finding","fields":{"check_id":"double_dipping","citations":["Kriegeskorte et al. 2009, Nat Neurosci 12:535"],"fix":null,"metrics":{"clustering":[],"marker_test":["rank_genes_groups"]},"status":"needs_evidence","verdict":"the cell groups here appear to be discovered by clustering the SAME expression data you then used to test which genes mark them, with no safeguard against the circularity — this is the 'double-dipping' pattern. Choosing the groups to separate the cells and then testing those same cells makes the p-values look more significant than they really are, so they can't be trusted as marker significance (this says nothing about whether any individual marker is real — only that the numbers are miscalibrated). The evidence is suggestive, not conclusive, so I'm flagging it for review rather than blocking. Re-run the marker test on data independent of the clustering (count-splitting, a held-out set, or ClusterDE)."}}
new_findings.experimental_unit: {"__type__":"sc_referee.checks.base.Finding","fields":{"check_id":"experimental_unit","citations":["Squair et al. 2021, Nat Commun 12:5692","Zimmerman et al. 2021, Nat Commun 12:738"],"fix":null,"metrics":{"comparable":false,"effect_corr":null,"engine":"simple","n_replicates_per_arm":4,"powered":false,"powered_fraction":null,"sign_flips":0,"survival_rate":1.0,"survivors":0,"valid_reported_sig":0},"status":"needs_evidence","verdict":"I couldn't fairly compare your results to a recomputation: reported table has significant rows only (can't rebuild the FDR family)"}}
new_findings.pairing: {"__type__":"sc_referee.checks.base.Finding","fields":{"check_id":"pairing","citations":["Zimmerman et al. 2021, Nat Commun 12:738","Squair et al. 2021, Nat Commun 12:5692"],"fix":null,"metrics":{"complete_pairs":4,"pairing_key":["donor_id"],"unmatched_levels":0},"status":"needs_evidence","verdict":"your model treats the samples as unpaired, but 4 subject(s) (values of donor_id) actually appear in BOTH groups — so the data is really paired. Ignoring that within-subject link can lose power or make the test too liberal; consider a paired (or mixed-model) sensitivity check."}}

intended_changes: experimental_unit: absent -> needs_evidence (confirmed per-cell test with exact donor-spanning contrast)
pairing: absent -> needs_evidence (four donor levels span both declared arms while the model is unpaired)
